title: Improve support of the '-p' option
author: jinxka
body:
Hello,

The nmap option '-p' allow us to specify whether we want the port to use TCP or UDP like:

`nmap -Pn -sU -sT -p U:102,T:502`

Would it be possible to upgrade the gem to follow this comportement?

Thanks

title: Add `# frozen_string_literal: true` to all files
author: postmodern

title: Switch to using `require_relative` to improve load-times
author: postmodern
body: Switch to using `require_relative` to load other `nmap/` files to improve load-times.

title: How to rewrite Nmap::Program(Nmap::Task.new
author: rgaufman
body:
Hi there,

I have this code:

```ruby
    Nmap::Command.find.sudo_task(Nmap::Task.new do |nmap|
      nmap.ping = true
      nmap.arp_ping = arp_ping
      nmap.skip_discovery = false
      nmap.disable_dns = true
      nmap.ipv6 = ip_address.ipv6?
      nmap.verbose = false
      nmap.quiet = true
      nmap.xml = temp_file.path
      nmap.targets = [ip]
    end)
```

Can you please shed how to write this to work in the latest verson? -- I tried changing Nmap::Program to Nmap::Command but it throws: 

```
NoMethodError: undefined method `find' for Nmap::Command:Class
```

title: Rename `nmap.raw_ip` to `nmap.send_ip`
author: postmodern
body: Rename `nmap.raw_ip` to `nmap.send_ip`, to match the `--send-ip` option.

title: Rename `nmap.raw_ethernet` to `nmap.send_eth`
author: postmodern
body: Rename `nmap.raw_ethernet` to `nmap.send_eth`, to match the `--send-eth` option.

title: Rename `nmap.disable_stylesheet` to `nmap.no_stylesheet`
author: postmodern
body: Rename `nmap.disable_stylesheet` to `nmap.no_stylesheet`, to match the `--no-stylesheet` option.

title: Rename `nmap.nmap_stylesheet` to `nmap.webxml`
author: postmodern
body: Rename `nmap.nmap_stylesheet` to `nmap.webxml` to match the `--webxml` option name.

title: Rename `nmap.append` to `nmap.append_output`
author: postmodern
body: Rename `nmap.append` to `nmap.append_output` to match the `--apend-output` option.

title: Allow passing a numeric value to `nmap.verbose`
author: postmodern
body: It should be possible to output options such as `-v1` or `-v2`.

title: Rename `nmap.grepable` to `nmap.output_grepable`
author: postmodern
body: Rename `nmap.grepable` to `nmap.output_grepable` to make it more obvious.

title: Rename `nmap.skiddie` to `nmap.output_skiddie`
author: postmodern
body: Rename `nmap.skiddie` to `nmap.ouput_skiddie` to make it more obvious.

title: Rename `nmap.xml` to `nmap.output_xml`
author: postmodern
body: Rename `nmap.xml` to `nmap.output_xml` to make it more obvious.

title: Rename `nmap.tcp_scan_flags` to just `nmap.scan_flags` to better match the option name
author: postmodern

title: Add `Nmap::Command::ScanFlags`
author: postmodern
body:
Add a custom type for the `--scanflags` option.

```
           The --scanflags argument can be a numerical flag value such as 9 (PSH and
           FIN), but using symbolic names is easier. Just mash together any combination
           of URG, ACK, PSH, RST, SYN, and FIN. For example, --scanflags
           URGACKPSHRSTSYNFIN sets everything, though it's not very useful for scanning.
           The order these are specified in is irrelevant.
```

title: Add `Nmap::Command::HexString`
author: postmodern
body:
Add a custom type `HexString` to validate the values given to `--data`.

```
       --data hex string (Append custom binary data to sent packets)
           This option lets you include binary data as payload in sent packets.  hex
           string may be specified in any of the following formats: 0xAABBCCDDEEFF...,
           AABBCCDDEEFF...  or \xAA\xBB\xCC\xDD\xEE\xFF.... Examples of use are --data
           0xdeadbeef and --data \xCA\xFE\x09. Note that if you specify a number like
           0x00ff no byte-order conversion is performed. Make sure you specify the
           information in the byte order expected by the receiver.
```

title: Add missing `--script-timeout` option
author: postmodern
body:
Map in the `--script-timeout` option.

```
       --script-timeout time
           While some scripts complete in fractions of a second, others can take hours
           or more depending on the nature of the script, arguments passed in, network
           and application conditions, and more. The --script-timeout option sets a
           ceiling on script execution time. Any script instance which exceeds that time
           will be terminated and no output will be shown. If debugging (-d) is enabled,
           Nmap will report on each timeout. For host and service scripts, a script
           instance only scans a single target host or port and the timeout period will
           be reset for the next instance.

           The special value 0 can be used to mean “no timeout”, which can be used to
           override the T5 timing template, which sets the script timeout to 10 minutes.
```

title: Add missing `--initial-rtt-timeout` option
author: postmodern
body:
Map in the `--initial-rtt-timeout` option.

```
--initial-rtt-timeout time
       (Adjust probe timeouts)
           Nmap maintains a running timeout value for determining how long it will wait
           for a probe response before giving up or retransmitting the probe. This is
           calculated based on the response times of previous probes.

           If the network latency shows itself to be significant and variable, this
           timeout can grow to several seconds. It also starts at a conservative (high)
           level and may stay that way for a while when Nmap scans unresponsive hosts.

           Specifying a lower --max-rtt-timeout and --initial-rtt-timeout than the
           defaults can cut scan times significantly. This is particularly true for
           pingless (-Pn) scans, and those against heavily filtered networks. Don't get
           too aggressive though. The scan can end up taking longer if you specify such
           a low value that many probes are timing out and retransmitting while the
           response is in transit.
```
